lessons.title: Sound as a Pressure Wave
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Clap your hands. You hear it instantly — but what actually traveled from your hands to your ears? Not the hands, and not really 'a sound object.' What travels is a tiny squeeze-and-stretch of the air.

**Build.** Air is made of countless tiny particles (molecules) zipping around. When something vibrates — a guitar string, a drum skin, your vocal cords — it shoves the air next to it. That shove squeezes the nearby air particles together into a **compression** (a spot of slightly higher pressure, where 'pressure' just means how hard the air is pushing). The vibrating object then pulls back, leaving the air there spread thin — a **rarefaction** (a spot of slightly lower pressure). Compression, rarefaction, compression, rarefaction: this pattern passes from particle to particle outward, like a whispered message handed down a line of people. That moving pattern is a **sound wave**.

Sound is called a **longitudinal wave**, meaning the air particles jiggle back and forth *along* the same direction the wave travels (not side to side). Importantly, the particles themselves don't fly across the room; each only nudges its neighbor and springs back. What moves across the room is the *pattern* of pressure.

Because sound needs particles to pass the squeeze along, it needs a **medium** — matter such as air, water, or steel. In the **vacuum** of outer space (no matter at all), there is nothing to compress, so sound cannot travel. Space is silent.

**Recap.** Sound is a longitudinal wave of compressions and rarefactions traveling through a medium. No medium, no sound.

items.prompt: What physically travels from a clapping hand to your ear?
choices: Tiny pieces of the hand flying across the room
A moving pattern of squeezed and spread-out air (a pressure wave)
A stream of air that blows steadily into your ear
Nothing at all; hearing happens only inside the brain
items.explanation: Sound is a traveling pattern of high- and low-pressure regions in the air. No pieces of the hand travel (choice 1), and it is not a steady wind blowing across (choice 3) — the air particles just jiggle and spring back. Something real does travel, so choice 4 is wrong.

items.prompt: A 'compression' in a sound wave is a region where the air is:
choices: Squeezed together, at slightly higher pressure
Spread thin, at slightly lower pressure
Completely removed, leaving a vacuum
Moving sideways across the wave's path
items.explanation: A compression is where particles are pushed close together, raising the pressure. The spread-thin, lower-pressure region is the rarefaction (choice 2). The air is never fully removed (choice 3), and the jiggling is along the wave's direction, not sideways (choice 4).

items.prompt: Why can't sound travel through the vacuum of outer space?
choices: Because space is too cold for sound to exist
Because there are no particles of matter to pass the compression along
Because sound is blocked by sunlight in space
Because sound travels only in straight lines and space curves
items.explanation: Sound needs a medium — particles that can be squeezed and pass the squeeze to their neighbors. A vacuum has essentially no particles, so there is nothing to carry the wave. Temperature (choice 1), sunlight (choice 3), and curvature (choice 4) are not the reason.

items.prompt: Sound is called a 'longitudinal' wave because the air particles:
choices: Move at right angles (sideways) to the wave's direction
Travel all the way across the room with the wave
Jiggle back and forth along the same direction the wave travels
Stay perfectly still while energy somehow passes through them
items.explanation: In a longitudinal wave the particle motion is parallel to (along) the travel direction. Sideways motion (choice 1) describes a transverse wave instead. The particles only nudge neighbors and spring back rather than crossing the room (choice 2), and they must move at least a little to pass the wave (choice 4).

items.prompt: A 'medium' for sound is best described as:
choices: The matter (such as air, water, or steel) that the wave travels through
The loudness setting on a speaker
The empty space between particles
A special material that only musical instruments contain
items.explanation: A medium is any matter whose particles can carry the compressions — air, water, metal, and so on. It is not a volume control (choice 2), not the empty gaps (choice 3), and not exclusive to instruments (choice 4).

items.prompt: When a sound wave passes through air, an individual air particle mostly:
choices: Is carried far across the room in the direction of the sound
Vibrates back and forth near its starting spot, then settles
Heats up and turns into a new particle
Stops moving entirely once the sound arrives
items.explanation: Each particle just oscillates around its home position and passes the disturbance onward, like people in a line nudging the next person. Particles are not transported across the room (choice 1); they are not created or destroyed (choice 3) and do not freeze (choice 4).

items.prompt: Which everyday situation best shows that sound needs a medium?
choices: A bell rings louder in a large hall than a small room
An alarm clock sealed in a jar grows silent as the air is pumped out
A drum sounds different from a flute
Thunder sounds deeper than a whistle
items.explanation: Removing the air removes the medium, so the sound fades to silence — directly showing sound needs matter. Room size affects echoes (choice 1), and choices 3 and 4 are about timbre and pitch, not whether a medium exists.

items.prompt: What creates a sound wave in the first place?
choices: A steady, unchanging object sitting still
Something vibrating, which repeatedly pushes and pulls the nearby air
A bright light shining on the air
Cooling the air down quickly
items.explanation: A vibrating object (string, drum skin, vocal cords) alternately compresses and rarefies the surrounding air, launching the wave. A still object makes no sound (choice 1); light (choice 3) and cooling (choice 4) do not by themselves create the back-and-forth pressure pattern.

lessons.title: Pitch, Loudness & Human Hearing
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Why does a tiny whistle sound 'high' and a big tuba sound 'low'? And why can a whisper and a shout both be the same note, yet one is barely there and the other rattles the windows? Two separate properties of a sound wave answer these: pitch and loudness.

**Build.** **Pitch** is how high or low a note sounds. Pitch is set by the wave's **frequency** — the number of compressions passing a point each second, measured in **hertz (Hz)**, where 1 Hz means one wave per second. More waves per second (higher frequency) = higher pitch. A whistle packs many waves into each second; a tuba sends few.

**Loudness** is how strong or faint a sound is. Loudness is set by the wave's **amplitude** — the size of the pressure swing, or how much the air is squeezed and stretched. Bigger amplitude = louder. A key beginner trap: loudness and pitch are independent. Turning a sound up makes it louder, not higher; you can play one note softly or loudly without changing the note.

Loudness is measured on the **decibel (dB)** scale. It is a *compressed* scale: every 10 dB step sounds roughly twice as loud, so a 90 dB motorcycle is far more than '3 times' a 30 dB whisper.

Human ears detect roughly **20 Hz to 20,000 Hz (20 kHz)**. Below 20 Hz is **infrasound** (felt more than heard — some elephants use it). Above 20 kHz is **ultrasound**, which bats and medical scanners use.

**Recap.** Pitch comes from frequency; loudness from amplitude; they are independent. Humans hear about 20 Hz to 20 kHz.

items.prompt: The pitch of a sound (how high or low it sounds) is determined by its:
choices: Amplitude
Frequency
Decibel level
Distance from the listener
items.explanation: Pitch is set by frequency — waves per second. Amplitude (choice 1) sets loudness, not pitch. Decibels (choice 3) measure loudness too. Distance affects how loud it seems, not the pitch.

items.prompt: You turn the volume up on a speaker playing a single steady note. What changes about the wave?
choices: Its frequency increases, so the note gets higher
Its amplitude increases, so the sound gets louder
Its wavelength shrinks, so the note gets higher
Nothing measurable changes about the wave
items.explanation: Volume controls amplitude (the size of the pressure swing), which sets loudness. The frequency — and therefore the pitch — stays the same, so choices 1 and 3 are the classic 'louder = higher' mistake. Something clearly changes, ruling out choice 4.

items.prompt: One hertz (1 Hz) means:
choices: One wave (cycle) passing a point each second
One decibel of loudness
One meter of wavelength
One unit of pressure in the air
items.explanation: Hertz counts cycles per second, the unit of frequency. Decibels (choice 2) measure loudness, meters (choice 3) measure wavelength, and pressure (choice 4) has its own units. Only choice 1 defines hertz.

items.prompt: A tuba plays a low note and a piccolo plays a high note. Compared with the piccolo, the tuba's sound wave has:
choices: A higher frequency (more waves per second)
A lower frequency (fewer waves per second)
A larger amplitude, which is what makes it low
Exactly the same frequency, just more decibels
items.explanation: Low pitch means low frequency — fewer compressions per second. Higher frequency (choice 1) would sound higher, like the piccolo. Amplitude (choice 3) is about loudness, not lowness, and identical frequencies (choice 4) would mean the same pitch.

items.prompt: The approximate range of frequencies a typical human can hear is:
choices: About 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz
About 1 Hz to 100 Hz
About 50,000 Hz to 100,000 Hz
There is no limit; humans hear all frequencies
items.explanation: Human hearing spans roughly 20 Hz (very low rumble) to 20 kHz (very high). The other ranges are too narrow (choice 2), entirely ultrasonic and inaudible to us (choice 3), or simply false — our ears do have limits (choice 4).

items.prompt: Sound with a frequency above 20,000 Hz, too high for humans to hear, is called:
choices: Infrasound
Ultrasound
Amplitude
Decibel sound
items.explanation: 'Ultra' means above: ultrasound is above the human range and is used by bats and medical scanners. Infrasound (choice 1) is below 20 Hz. Amplitude (choice 3) and 'decibel sound' (choice 4) are not frequency categories.

items.prompt: Why is the decibel (dB) scale described as a 'compressed' scale?
choices: Because it only works for compressed air
Because each 10 dB step sounds roughly twice as loud, so small numbers cover huge loudness ranges
Because it measures how compressed the air particles are
Because louder sounds always have lower decibel numbers
items.explanation: The decibel scale grows in big jumps — about every 10 dB doubles perceived loudness — so 30 dB and 90 dB differ enormously. It is not limited to compressed air (choice 1), does not directly count particle squeeze (choice 3), and louder means higher dB, not lower (choice 4).

items.prompt: A whisper and a shout can carry the exact same musical note. This shows that:
choices: Loudness and pitch are the same thing
Pitch (frequency) and loudness (amplitude) are independent properties
A shout always has a higher pitch than a whisper
Only loud sounds have a definite pitch
items.explanation: Same note means same frequency (pitch), while whisper vs shout differ in amplitude (loudness) — proving the two are independent. They are not identical (choice 1); shouting raises volume, not pitch (choice 3); and quiet sounds have pitch too (choice 4).

lessons.title: The Speed of Sound
lessons.content:
**Hook.** During a thunderstorm you see the lightning flash, then count 'one-one-thousand, two-one-thousand...' before the thunder rumbles in. That gap is a clue: sound takes real time to travel, and it is far slower than light.

**Build.** In ordinary air near room temperature, sound travels at about **343 meters per second (m/s)** — roughly 1,235 kilometers per hour. Fast for us, but light is almost a million times faster, arriving essentially instantly. So during a storm the light reaches you at once while the sound lags; the longer the gap, the farther away the lightning struck (very roughly, sound covers about 1 kilometer every 3 seconds).

The speed of sound depends on the **medium** — the matter it moves through. Here is the key, and a common surprise: sound travels **faster in solids and liquids than in air**, not slower. Why? In a solid like steel, particles are packed tightly and strongly linked, so a push is handed to the next particle almost immediately. In a gas the particles are far apart, so the message dawdles. Rough speeds: air about 343 m/s, water about 1,480 m/s, steel about 5,000 m/s. (A beginner often guesses 'denser = slower,' but tighter, stiffer bonds actually speed sound up.)

We can connect speed, frequency, and wavelength with $v = f\\lambda$, read as 'speed equals frequency times wavelength,' where $v$ is speed, $f$ is frequency (waves per second), and $\\lambda$ (the Greek letter lambda) is the wavelength — the length of one full wave.

**Recap.** Sound moves about 343 m/s in air and faster in liquids and solids; that is why thunder lags lightning.

items.prompt: The approximate speed of sound in air at room temperature is:
choices: About 343 meters per second
About 3 meters per second
About 300,000,000 meters per second
Sound has no fixed speed in air
items.explanation: Sound in air is about 343 m/s. Three meters per second (choice 2) is walking pace, far too slow. 300 million m/s (choice 3) is the speed of light, not sound. Sound does have a definite speed in a given medium (choice 4).

items.prompt: In which medium does sound travel FASTEST?
choices: Air
Water
Steel
Sound travels at the same speed in all of them
items.explanation: Sound is fastest in steel (~5000 m/s) because its tightly bonded particles pass the disturbance along quickly. Air (~343 m/s) is slowest and water (~1480 m/s) is in between. The speed clearly differs by medium, ruling out choice 4.

items.prompt: Why do you see lightning before you hear thunder, even though they happen together?
choices: Light is created a few seconds before the sound
Light travels vastly faster than sound, so it arrives first
Thunder bounces off clouds and is delayed by the echo
Your eyes simply react faster than your ears
items.explanation: Light covers the distance almost instantly while sound crawls at ~343 m/s, so the flash beats the rumble. They are produced at the same instant (choice 1 is wrong), the lag is the travel time, not echoes (choice 3), and it is the wave speeds, not reaction times, that matter (choice 4).

items.prompt: A common misconception is that sound travels slower in solids than in air. The truth is that sound is faster in solids because:
choices: Solids are heavier, and heavy things move faster
A solid's tightly packed, strongly bonded particles pass the disturbance to neighbors almost instantly
Solids contain no particles to slow the sound down
Sound in a solid turns into light and speeds up
items.explanation: Tight, stiff bonds hand the push to the next particle very quickly, so sound races through solids. Weight alone is not the reason (choice 1); solids are full of particles (choice 3); and sound does not become light (choice 4).

items.prompt: If you count about 6 seconds between a lightning flash and its thunder, the lightning was roughly:
choices: About 2 kilometers away
About 100 kilometers away
Right next to you
Distance cannot be estimated this way at all
items.explanation: Sound covers about 1 km every 3 seconds, so 6 seconds means roughly 2 km. 100 km (choice 2) would take far longer; a 6-second gap means it is not right next to you (choice 3); and the timing method genuinely works as a rough estimate (choice 4).

items.prompt: In the equation $v = f\\lambda$, the symbol $\\lambda$ (lambda) stands for:
choices: The loudness of the sound
The wavelength — the length of one full wave
The number of waves per second
The speed of the wave
items.explanation: Lambda is the wavelength, the length of a single complete wave. Loudness (choice 1) is not in this equation; waves per second is the frequency $f$ (choice 3); and the speed is $v$ (choice 4).

items.prompt: Compared with the speed of light, the speed of sound in air is:
choices: Slightly faster
About the same
Far slower
Exactly half as fast
items.explanation: Light travels about 300 million m/s versus sound's ~343 m/s — sound is hundreds of thousands of times slower. It is nowhere near faster, equal, or half (choices 1, 2, 4).

items.prompt: Two friends tap a long metal pipe; one listens with an ear pressed to the metal, the other listens through the air the same distance away. What happens?
choices: The listener on the metal hears the tap first, because sound is faster in the solid
The listener in the air hears it first, because air is faster
They hear it at exactly the same instant
Neither hears anything, because metal blocks sound
items.explanation: Sound speeds through steel (~5000 m/s) far faster than through air (~343 m/s), so the metal listener hears it first. Air is the slower medium (choice 2), the speeds differ so the timing differs (choice 3), and metal carries sound well rather than blocking it (choice 4).

lessons.title: Doppler Effect, Resonance & Beats
lessons.content:
**Hook.** A siren races past: as it approaches the pitch sounds high, and the instant it passes, the pitch drops — 'eeeeee-ooooow.' The driver inside hears no change at all. What gives?

**Build.** This is the **Doppler effect**: when a sound source moves toward you, each new wave is sent from a little closer than the last, so the compressions arrive bunched together — more waves per second, meaning a higher *perceived* frequency, so higher pitch. As the source moves away, the waves are stretched apart, fewer arrive per second, and you hear a lower pitch. Crucial point and a common trap: the source's *true* frequency never changes — only the frequency you *perceive* changes because of the motion. The driver, moving with the siren, hears it normally.

Now, how do instruments make steady musical notes? Through **standing waves** and **resonance**. A guitar string or the air in a flute can only vibrate comfortably at certain special frequencies set by its length; at those frequencies the reflected waves reinforce each other into a stable, fixed pattern called a **standing wave**. **Resonance** is this natural strong response at a favored frequency — it is why a short string or short pipe gives a high note and a long one gives a low note.

Finally, **beats**: play two notes whose frequencies are *almost* equal and you hear a slow throbbing 'wah-wah-wah' as they alternately reinforce and cancel. Musicians use beats to tune — the throb slows and vanishes when the two pitches match exactly.

**Recap.** Doppler shifts *perceived* pitch with motion (source unchanged); standing waves and resonance let instruments make set notes; near-equal frequencies make beats.

items.prompt: As an ambulance siren moves toward you, you hear its pitch as:
choices: Lower than normal
Higher than normal
Exactly normal, with no change
Silent until it passes
items.explanation: An approaching source bunches its waves, so more arrive per second and the perceived pitch rises. A receding source would sound lower (choice 1). Motion does change the perceived pitch (choices 3 and 4 are wrong).

items.prompt: In the Doppler effect, what actually changes when a siren approaches and then recedes?
choices: The true frequency produced by the siren itself
Only the frequency perceived by the listener; the source's true frequency is unchanged
Both the true frequency and the loudness disappear
The speed of sound in the air around the siren
items.explanation: The siren keeps emitting the same frequency; motion changes how often waves reach the listener, shifting the perceived pitch. The source frequency does not actually change (choice 1), nor does sound's speed in the medium (choice 4); choice 3 misstates what happens.

items.prompt: Why does the driver inside the ambulance hear the siren at a steady, normal pitch?
choices: Because the driver is moving along with the source, so the waves are not bunched or stretched for them
Because the siren shuts off its Doppler effect for the driver
Because the inside of the ambulance is a vacuum
Because the driver's ears cancel out the change
items.explanation: There is no relative motion between the driver and the source, so no Doppler shift occurs for them. The effect cannot be 'switched off' (choice 2), the cab is full of air (choice 3), and ears do not auto-cancel pitch shifts (choice 4).

items.prompt: As a sound source moves AWAY from a listener, the waves reaching that listener are:
choices: Bunched closer together, raising the pitch
Stretched farther apart, lowering the pitch
Unchanged in spacing
Stopped completely
items.explanation: A receding source leaves each wave a bit farther behind, stretching them out so fewer arrive per second — a lower perceived pitch. Bunching and higher pitch (choice 1) happen when approaching. The spacing does change (choice 3), and the sound does not stop (choice 4).

items.prompt: A 'standing wave' on a guitar string is:
choices: A wave that travels off the end of the string into the room
A stable, fixed vibration pattern formed when reflected waves reinforce at certain frequencies
A string that is not moving at all
The loudness of the plucked note
items.explanation: At special frequencies set by the string's length, waves reflecting back and forth reinforce into a steady pattern that appears to 'stand still.' It is not a wave leaving the string (choice 1), not a motionless string (choice 3), and not loudness (choice 4).

items.prompt: On a guitar, pressing a string shorter (so a smaller length vibrates) produces:
choices: A lower note, because shorter means slower
A higher note, because shorter lengths resonate at higher frequencies
The same note, because length does not matter
A louder note, but the same pitch
items.explanation: Shorter vibrating lengths resonate at higher frequencies, giving higher notes — that is how fretting works. Shorter does not mean lower (choice 1); length strongly affects pitch (choice 3); and the change is in pitch, not just loudness (choice 4).

items.prompt: 'Resonance' in a musical instrument refers to:
choices: An object's natural strong vibration at certain favored frequencies
The decibel level of the loudest possible note
The Doppler shift heard when the instrument moves
The time it takes a note to fade to silence
items.explanation: Resonance is the large, sustained response a system gives at its preferred (natural) frequencies, which is how strings and air columns build clear notes. It is not a loudness measure (choice 2), not Doppler motion (choice 3), and not the fade time (choice 4).

items.prompt: When two notes of ALMOST equal frequency play together, you hear a slow throbbing called 'beats' because the waves:
choices: Travel at different speeds through the air
Alternately reinforce (get louder) and cancel (get softer) as they drift in and out of step
Change the speed of sound around them
Merge into a single much higher note
items.explanation: Slightly mismatched frequencies repeatedly fall into and out of step, so the combined sound swells and fades — the throb we call beats, useful for tuning. The notes travel at the same speed (choice 1), do not alter sound's speed (choice 3), and do not fuse into one higher note (choice 4).
